pattern: Binary-search the answer time T; a worker with per-unit cost t can chip away x units within T seconds where t*x*(x+1)/2 <= T, so summing each worker's capacity at a candidate T turns 'is T enough' into a monotone feasibility check.
hints: If you fix a total time budget T, you can compute exactly how many units of height any single worker could remove within that budget -- the cost of their x-th unit grows linearly, so their total cost for x units is triangular.
The total height removable by the whole crew within budget T is monotone non-decreasing in T, so binary search on T for the smallest budget that lets the crew reach the required total.

For a fixed worker time t and budget T, the max units x satisfies t*x*(x+1) <= 2*T -- solve it directly (integer sqrt) or binary-search x, then sum across workers and compare against the mountain height.

edge_inputs.description: single worker, single unit to remove
minimum height and minimum worker time
max height with the single fastest possible worker
one very fast worker among very slow ones -- optimal crew barely uses the slow ones
several identical workers must split the load evenly
many identical slow workers, small height
large worker roster, all at the max time value

stress.description: random height with a random-sized worker roster of varied speeds
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    height = rng.randint(1, max(2, min(size, 3000)))
    w = rng.randint(1, 300)
    workerTimes = [rng.randint(1, 100000) for _ in range(w)]
    return [height, workerTimes]
